Create a Discord application, copy your bot token, add it to your OpenClaw configuration, and invite the bot to your server. # OpenClaw x Discord Server Integration Guide. Architecture diagram of OpenClaw integrated with Discord server. After embedding an OpenClaw AI agent into a Discord server, your community gains the following capabilities:. Compared to Telegram's personal remote control scenario or Slack's enterprise workflow scenario, the Discord x OpenClaw integration is best suited for **community management, open-source project support, and large-scale multi-user interaction**. Step 1: Create a Discord Bot and Obtain Token. This is the foundation of the entire **OpenClaw Discord setup** process -- creating an Application and Bot on the Discord Developer Portal and obtaining the necessary authentication Token. ### 3.1 Create a Discord Application. ### 3.2 Create Bot and Obtain Token. **Important security reminder:** The Bot Token is equivalent to full access credentials for the Bot -- anyone who has it can control your Bot. Never expose the Token in public places (GitHub, Discord channels, screenshots). After obtaining the Discord Bot Token, the next step is to write it into OpenClaw's configuration so the Gateway can connect to Discord as your Bot.

title: 🦞 Discord - OpenClaw
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

# Discord. OpenClaw connects to Discord as a bot over the official Discord gateway. DMs and guild channels are supported. Discord DMs default to pairing mode. Create a Discord application with a bot, add the bot to your server, and pair it with OpenClaw. Use a private server if you can; create one first (**Create My Own > For me and my friends**) if needed. - ### Create a Discord application and bot. In the Discord Developer Portal, click **New Application** and name it (for example "OpenClaw"). Open **Bot** in the sidebar and set the **Username** to your agent's name. If the bot will post in threads — including forum or media channel workflows that create or continue a thread — also enable **Send Messages in Threads**. If your host is blocked or rate-limited by Discord's startup application lookup, set the application/client ID from the Developer Portal so startup can skip that REST call: `channels.discord.applicationId` for the default account, or `channels.discord.accounts..applicationId` per bot.

title: 🦞 Multi-Channel Bots with OpenClaw: Telegram, Discord & Slack - Learn OpenClaw - Resource Center
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

# Multi-Channel Bots with OpenClaw: Telegram, Discord & Slack. Imagine this: Your support team gets questions on Telegram. Your dev team lives in Discord. Your sales team uses Slack. Instead of running three separate chatbots (and three separate AI bills), you run **one OpenClaw agent** that responds intelligently across all three platforms simultaneously. OpenClaw’s `message` tool supports multiple channel plugins out of the box. In this ultimate guide, you’ll master everything from basic setup to advanced multi-agent architectures, security hardening, and production deployment strategies. By the end of this guide, you’ll have a production-ready multi-channel system that can:. * Receive and route messages intelligently across platforms. * Bind user identities across channels for unified conversations. Your team might live in Discord. Your agent doesn’t care whether the message came from Telegram or Discord—it just responds. If you’re hosting on a VPS (recommended for production), this means uptime, predictable networking, and isolation from your personal machine. * Bot tokens from each platform you want to support.

title: 🦞 OpenClaw: Scheduled tasks
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

Cron is the Gateway's built-in scheduler. It persists jobs, wakes the agent at the right time, and can deliver output to a chat channel, a webhook, or nowhere. `openclaw cron create "2027-02-01T16:00:00Z" \ --name "Reminder" \ --session main \ --system-event "Reminder: check the cron docs draft" \ --wake now \ --delete-after-run`. `openclaw cron listopenclaw cron get <job-id>openclaw cron show <job-id>`. `openclaw cron runs --id <job-id>`. Cron task reconciliation is runtime-owned first, durable-history-backed second: an active cron task stays live while the cron runtime still tracks that job as running, even if an old child session row still exists. Once the runtime stops owning the job and a 5-minute grace window expires, maintenance checks persisted run logs and job state for the matching `cron:<jobId>:<startedAt>` run. To require both conditions, use croner's `+` day-of-week modifier (`0 9 15 * +1`), or schedule on one field and guard the other in your job's prompt or command. On `cron edit`, removes the per-job model override so the job follows normal cron model precedence (stored cron-session override, else agent/default model).

title: 🦞 How to Set Up OpenClaw Cron Jobs (With Examples You Can Copy) | Stack Junkie
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

# How to Set Up OpenClaw Cron Jobs (With Examples You Can Copy). Set up OpenClaw cron jobs with openclaw cron add. Three schedule types, copy-paste templates for backups, reminders, and health checks, plus how to debug failed runs. Create a cron job: tell your agent "remind me every morning at 9am" or use `openclaw cron add`. Three schedule types: `at` (one-shot), `every` (interval), and `cron` (standard expressions like `0 9 * * *`). Jobs run in isolated sessions by default (fresh context, recommended) or the main session. Cron expressions use your Gateway host's local timezone. | `0 7 * * *` | Every day at 7:00 AM (host timezone) |. | `0 7 * * 1-5` | Weekdays at 7:00 AM (host timezone) |. Each row shows the job ID (use this for other commands), name, schedule expression, enabled state, last run timestamp, and last run status. Use `openclaw cron list` to find the job ID.

title: 🦞 OpenClaw Cron Scheduled Tasks Guide | MI
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

# OpenClaw Cron Scheduled Tasks Guide. Diagram of OpenClaw Cron scheduled task automation execution. What Is OpenClaw's Cron? If you're familiar with Linux's `crontab`, OpenClaw's Cron is conceptually similar -- it automatically executes tasks at specified times. But the key difference is: traditional crontab executes fixed Shell commands, while OpenClaw's Cron executes **natural language instructions**, with the AI agent autonomously deciding how to complete them. `"Every day at 9 AM, check server status and send a summary to Telegram"`. The latter doesn't require you to write scripts in advance -- the agent will decide which check commands to execute, how to organize the results, and how to format the report on its own. `openclaw cron add --schedule "0 9 * * *" --message "Check the server's CPU and memory usage. If a Cron task's execution time exceeds this value, the Gateway will forcibly terminate the connection, causing the task to be truncated -- the agent may have completed 80% of the work, but cannot return results due to the timeout.

That’s what the built-in cron scheduler is for. It runs inside the Gateway process so you don’t need a separate job runner and it can deliver results straight back to Telegram WhatsApp Slack Discord and more. This isn’t just “run a script at 7 AM.” It’s “wake the agent run a prompt or drop a system event into the main session then deliver output where I will actually see it.”. OpenClaw cron is a scheduler that lives inside the Gateway. You can send the same job output to a private Telegram chat and also to a Slack channel if you want. Another one is a weekly review that runs in a clean isolated session and only posts the final result back to you.

title: 🦞 OpenClaw Multi-Agent Collaboration | MI - 超智諮詢
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

# OpenClaw Multi-Agent Collaboration. OpenClaw multi-agent collaboration architecture: SubAgent and Agent Teams communication diagram. When your AI agent needs to simultaneously crawl web pages, analyze data, generate reports, and review code — a single agent simply cannot handle it all. This is the eighteenth article in the OpenClaw series, focusing on the complete technical architecture of **OpenClaw multi-agent collaboration**. We will break down the three core mechanisms — SubAgent (child agents), Agent Teams, and AgentToAgent (cross-agent communication) — examining their design principles, configuration methods, and applicable scenarios one by one. We also provide ready-to-use `openclaw.json` multi-agent configuration examples, along with performance tuning and security best practices. Before diving into the technical details of OpenClaw multiagent, let us first clarify a fundamental question: when do you actually need to upgrade from a single agent to a multi-agent architecture? OpenClaw provides three clearly tiered multi-agent collaboration mechanisms, from simple to complex: **SubAgent** (child agents), **Agent Teams** (team collaboration), and **AgentToAgent** (cross-agent communication).
